summary: Dan Luu systematically dismantles the common startup mythology by analyzing compensation, work quality, and career development at big tech companies versus startups. Using concrete numbers, he demonstrates that senior engineers at major tech companies can earn a lifetime's median US income in just 6-7 years ($250k+/year), while startup equity has roughly a 0.5% chance of meaningful payoff for early employees. He argues that big companies now offer both superior compensation and access to technically interesting problems at unprecedented scale, though acknowledges the trade-offs vary by individual interests and circumstances.
key_points: Big tech companies offer dramatically higher compensation than commonly believed - $250k+/year for senior engineers with high probability versus lottery-ticket odds at startups
The startup compensation advantage has eroded significantly since 2004; equity typically becomes worthless or requires staying past desired departure due to ISO expiration and tax implications
Interesting technical work at scale is increasingly concentrated at big companies, which have resources for speculative 9-figure research projects that startups cannot match
The real differences are between individual companies, teams, and managers rather than between company types - picking the right specific opportunity matters more than the category
You can get startup upside while working at a big company by investing $25k/year in seed rounds, getting better terms than employee equity without vesting requirements
money_quotes: Roll d100... 0~70: Your equity grant is worth nothing. 71~94: Your equity grant is worth a lump sum of money which makes you about as much money as you gave up working for the startup. 95~99: Your equity grant is a life changing amount of money. 100: You worked at the next Google, and are rich beyond the dreams of avarice. Perceptive readers will note that 100 does not actually show up on a d100.

The only difference between then and now is that I took a job at a big company. When I took that job, the common story I heard at orientation was basically 'I never thought I'd be able to get a job at Google, but a recruiter emailed me and I figured I might as well respond'.

Since the company is a $X billion dollar a year company, the project only needed to move the needle by a small percentage to be worth it. And so a research project whose goal was to speed up the progress of another research project was approved.

The differences between types of companies is smaller than the differences between companies of the same type. This is recursive. The differences between different managers and teams at a company can easily be larger than the differences between companies.

A lot of people are really invested in the idea that they're making about as much as possible. If you've decided that making less money is the right trade-off for you, that's fine and I don't have any problem with that. But if you really think that you can't make that much money and you don't believe me, I recommend talking to one of the hundreds of thousands of engineers at one of the many large companies that pays well.
key_insight: The conventional wisdom that startups offer better compensation and more interesting work than big tech companies is outdated - the 2010s tech boom has inverted the risk-reward calculation, with big companies now offering both higher expected value compensation and access to technically challenging problems at unprecedented scale.
